title: Operational Temperature

content.statement: Every organization runs at a certain heat level, and too much heat destroys judgment.
content.question: What's your business's operational temperature this week? Too hot, too cold, or productive?
content.equation: OT = (Urgency + Firefighting + Stress + Volatility + Context Switching) ÷ 5
sop: Track how many fires (unplanned urgent interruptions) hit your team this week and what caused each one.
Trace each fire back to its root cause, most are preventable with better planning, not faster reacting.
Rate Urgency, Firefighting, Stress, Volatility, and Context Switching 1-10 for the week (see calculation, page 5)
If temperature is running high, cancel or delay one non-urgent commitment to create breathing room.
Re-check weekly, a team that's always hot is a team that's always making worse decisions.
content.explainer: Some heat focuses the team. Too much destroys it. You're the thermostat, know when to cool things down.
questions: What's your business's operational temperature this week? Too hot, too cold, or productive?
How many times did your team switch from planned work to putting out a fire this week?
Is the urgency your team feels coming from real deadlines, or from a lack of planning upstream?
content.equation_example: Rate Urgency, Firefighting, Stress, Volatility, and Context Switching 1-10 each. Urgency 8, Firefighting 7, Stress 6, Volatility 5, Context Switching 7 → OT = (8+7+6+5+7)/5 = 6.6 out of 10. Higher OT means the team is running hot and judgment is degrading; lower means there's room to think clearly, though too low can mean a lack of urgency.
content.closing_statement: Heat focuses you until it starts to blind you.

caption:
Principle Nº 26: Operational Temperature

Every organization runs at a certain heat level, and too much heat destroys judgment. Some heat focuses the team.

Ask yourself: What's your business's operational temperature this week? Too hot, too cold, or productive?
